evals.prompt: Document our `deploy` CLI command as a reference: all its arguments, flags, defaults, and exit codes.
evals.expected_output: An information-oriented Diataxis reference: a neutral subject line, a synopsis, and uniform tables describing every argument and option with name, type, default, and constraints, plus an exit-code table.

deterministic_checks.description: Includes a synopsis/usage line naming the deploy command (fenced, inline-coded, or plain)
Options are tabulated with name/type/default columns
An exit-code table or section is present
Frontmatter declares type: semantic
Contains no numbered tutorial-style learning steps
Contains no 'we recommend' rationale/opinion language
Contains no 'best practice' rationale/opinion language

expectations: Opens with a neutral one-line statement of the single thing described, not a learning promise or greeting
Every option is described with the same fields in the same order — no entry is abbreviated relative to the others
Passes mif-validate at least at Level 1

evals.prompt: I wrote reference docs for our config file but a reviewer says they read like a tutorial. Show me what's wrong and a corrected version.
evals.expected_output: Identifies mode-mixing (tutorial steps, opinions, rationale inside a reference) and rewrites it as a dry, exhaustive, uniformly structured description.

deterministic_checks.description: Names the tutorial-mode violation explicitly
Names the rationale/opinion violation explicitly
States that reference is information-oriented
Corrected version does not contain a first/then narrative sequence

expectations: Distinguishes reference from tutorial and explanation as distinct Diataxis modes, not just 'more concise'
Produces a corrected reference with uniform per-field entries and a structure mirroring the config file's own layout
Moves rationale out to a linked explanation rather than inlining it in the corrected version

evals.prompt: Turn this OpenAPI endpoint definition into a human-readable reference page for the GET /orders endpoint.
evals.expected_output: A conformant reference describing the endpoint: request parameters, response schema fields, and status codes, each as a uniform table entry.

deterministic_checks.description: Subject line or synopsis names the concrete GET /orders endpoint
Parameters/fields are tabulated with name and type columns
A status-code table is present
Frontmatter declares type: semantic

expectations: Describes every parameter and response field uniformly with required/default and constraints, not just a subset
Document order mirrors the endpoint's own structure (path, parameters, then responses)
Contains no guided walkthrough of how to call the endpoint, only the facts about its shape

evals.prompt: Should our docs for the settings schema be a tutorial, a how-to, or reference? Users come to it to look up what each field means.
evals.expected_output: Recommends reference because the goal is fact lookup, and explains the Diataxis distinction.

deterministic_checks.description: Response names reference as the recommended genre
Response contrasts with tutorial
Response contrasts with how-to

expectations: Ties the recommendation specifically to fact-lookup intent, not a generic 'reference is more formal' argument
Contrasts tutorial as learning-by-doing and how-to as accomplishing a known task, distinct from lookup
Offers to draft the reference with uniform per-field entries mirroring the schema's own structure

evals.prompt: Here's our environment-variables list for the worker service — about 20 vars, half of them I'm not totally sure about the defaults for. Write a reference page for them anyway.
evals.expected_output: A reference that lists every variable uniformly, using an explicit 'none' or em-dash placeholder for unknown defaults rather than omitting the field or guessing, and does not fabricate values.

deterministic_checks.description: Uses an explicit placeholder for missing/unknown defaults rather than leaving them silent
Variables are tabulated with a default column
Flags to the user that some defaults are unconfirmed rather than silently guessing

expectations: Does not fabricate specific default values for the variables the user said were uncertain
Every variable in the list gets the same table columns, including the ones with unknown defaults

evals.prompt: Review my reference doc for the `mifx export` command — I think it's fine but wanted a second pair of eyes: 'Getting Started with mifx export. So you want to export your documents? Great — let's walk through it together. Step 1: First, run the command. Why we built it this way: We chose JSON-LD because it's best practice. Options: There are a bunch of useful flags, `--format` is probably the most important, `--out` is also handy.'
evals.expected_output: Identifies every Diataxis violation in the draft (tutorial title, learning-oriented tone, numbered steps, embedded rationale, vague non-exhaustive options) and rewrites it as a proper reference.

deterministic_checks.description: Flags the tutorial-style title as a violation
Flags the numbered 'Step 1' learning step as a violation
Flags the embedded rationale/opinion as a violation
Flags the vague, non-exhaustive options list as a violation
Rewritten version does not retain the conversational tutorial tone
The rewrite's heading is a neutral subject line naming the command, not a greeting

expectations: Names all four violation categories, not just one or two of them
The rewrite lists every flag mentioned uniformly instead of singling out '--format' as 'most important'
